a.why:
Verified at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. The fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py:_copy_scrubbed (lines 100-124) replaces the old `if srcf.suffix == ".jsonl"` gate — which copied every non-jsonl file (incl. .json workflow/subagent transcripts) RAW, bypassing redaction — with a decode-strict classification: UTF-8-decodable text is run through _scrub, genuinely binary files (UnicodeDecodeError) are copied verbatim. This correctly closes the .json PII/secret bypass.

APPROPRIATE: correct, no regression (jsonl still scrubbed; binary preserved byte-for-byte; the broad except-Exception provenance guard and manifest logic untouched), and idiomatic (try/except/else; strict decode is actually safer than the prior errors="replace" which could silently mangle bytes). The history filter-repo cleanup is correctly scoped out and documented; working-tree re-scrub is the in-scope deliverable.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: I reverted to the original suffix-gate code and re-ran. test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_non_jsonl_text_files FAILS on revert (leaks 'somebody' + bearer token from a .json file) and PASSES with the fix — a strong discriminator on the exact bypass. test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim passes both ways (binary was always copied raw) so it is a guard/documentation test, not a discriminator — acceptable since the first test carries the security-relevant discrimination. Both named tests pass on the committed code; full tests/test_scripts.py green (8 passed); file restored clean (git diff empty).

b.why: Fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py (_copy_scrubbed, lines 100-124) replaces the old `suffix == ".jsonl"` gate with decode-strict classification: any UTF-8-decodable file is passed through _scrub(); only genuinely undecodable bytes are copied verbatim. This closes the bypass where .json workflow/subagent transcripts (reached via rglob("*") in main(), line 144) were copied raw, leaking operator PII + credential-shaped secrets. APPROPRIATE: correct and idiomatic (try/except UnicodeDecodeError), no regression — dedup, manifest, and never-break-commit exception handling all preserved; .jsonl path still scrubbed. COMPLETE/DISCRIMINATING: I verified by reverting to the .jsonl-only gate — test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_non_jsonl_text_files FAILS (username 'somebody' and the bearer token leak into the .json output), and PASSES with the fix. All 8 tests in tests/test_scripts.py pass with the fix restored. Note: test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim does NOT itself discriminate against the old code (both versions copy binary unchanged) — it is a regression guard for the new decode path — but the security fix is fully covered by the discriminating scrub test, so completeness holds. Caveat acknowledged in the fix description: working tree is re-scrubbed by the pre-commit hook, but git history still requires filter-repo before publish (documented, out of scope for this code fix).

a.why:
Assessed against the committed HEAD (e3eb708) of /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py. (Note: a concurrent autonomous process was mutating the working tree during review, so I evaluated the canonical committed state and ran the discrimination test in an isolated git worktree to avoid the race.)

APPROPRIATE: In confirm_whole_corpus the signature default is now stability_threshold: float | None = None (was 0.9). Resolution is resolved_stability = _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.stability_threshold, stability_threshold, "stability_threshold"); stability_threshold = 0.9 if resolved_stability is None else resolved_stability (confirm.py:368-371). This is exactly locked-wins-else-supplied with a 0.9 fallback only when NEITHER is given — correct semantics. It mirrors the existing alpha handling and reuses the same _resolve_locked_param helper, so it is idiomatic. No regression: the downstream range guard (0 < stability_threshold <= 1) still runs on the resolved float, and the override-HARKing protection from H-001 is preserved (an explicitly supplied value that disagrees with a locked one still raises). The earlier H-001 fix had inadvertently made the fixed 0.9 default indistinguishable from a caller passing 0.9, so any hypothesis locking a threshold != 0.9 spuriously raised unless the caller restated it; the None sentinel cleanly resolves that follow-on regression.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: test_locked_stability_threshold_is_honored_without_restating_it (test_confirm.py:274) locks stability_threshold=0.6, omits the confirm-time arg, and asserts evidence==0.6 and label SUPPORTED. With the fix both this and test_locked_stability_threshold_cannot_be_overridden_at_confirm_time pass. Reverting the fix in an isolated worktree (signature back to float = 0.9, fixed default fed straight into _resolve_locked_param) made the named test FAIL with exactly the spurious "stability_threshold argument 0.9 contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered stability_threshold 0.6" ValueError, while the override-raises test still PASSED. So the test is a true discriminator and the override guard is independently retained.

b.why:
Fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py is correct, regression-free, and idiomatic. It changes confirm_whole_corpus's stability_threshold default from 0.9 to a None sentinel, resolves locked-wins-else-supplied via the existing _resolve_locked_param (which already returns float|None), and falls back to 0.9 only when neither locked nor supplied. This fixes the H-001 follow-on regression where the literal 0.9 default was passed as the "supplied" arg, so any hypothesis locking a threshold != 0.9 always raised a spurious "contradicts pre-registered" ValueError unless the caller restated it. The change mirrors the existing alpha-handling pattern and finally uses _resolve_locked_param's float|None return type properly (the old cast(float, ...) masked the sentinel problem).

Discrimination verified by reversion: I reverted both the signature default (back to float = 0.9) and the resolution block (back to cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(...))). With the old code, test_locked_stability_threshold_is_honored_without_restating_it FAILS with exactly "stability_threshold argument 0.9 contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered stability_threshold 0.6", while the override-raises test still passes (1 failed, 1 passed). Restoring the fix: both targeted tests pass and the full tests/test_confirm.py passes (44 passed). The named test therefore genuinely discriminates and the existing override-raises guard is preserved.

a.why:
Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py and tests/test_observability.py.

APPROPRIATE: _int_env(name, default) (lines 32-42) reads via os.environ.get(name, default), wraps int() in try/except catching ValueError and TypeError, and re-raises ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") with `from exc` chaining. TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006") (line 45) preserves the original default and import-time semantics, so no regression. The error now names the offending var, matching the cited #H-022 vectorstore pattern. Idiomatic.

COMPLETE/DISCRIMINATES: test_tracing_int_env_names_the_bad_var (lines 23-27) asserts pytest.raises(ValueError, match="ASSAY_TRACING_PORT must be an integer") when calling tr._int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "not-a-port"). Test passes under .venv/bin/python (1 passed). I verified the reverted bare int() raises "invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'not-a-port'", which does NOT contain "ASSAY_TRACING_PORT must be an integer" — so the match= regex would fail and the test would fail on revert. The test discriminates.

Minor (non-blocking): the test exercises _int_env directly rather than importing the module with a malformed ASSAY_TRACING_PORT, so the call-site wiring at line 45 is not directly asserted; but that is a trivial one-liner and the function is the unit under test.

b.why:
Fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py replaces a bare import-time int() with _int_env(name, default), which raises ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") naming the offending var, catches ValueError and TypeError, chains via 'from exc', and preserves the default ("6006") — so valid-input behavior is unchanged. TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006"). Idiomatic and consistent with the cited #H-022 vectorstore fix class. No regression: full collection works under the .venv (py3.9 fails only due to unrelated dataclass slots needing 3.10+).

Test test_tracing_int_env_names_the_bad_var (tests/test_observability.py:23) calls tr._int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "not-a-port") and asserts pytest.raises(ValueError, match="ASSAY_TRACING_PORT must be an integer"). Verified PASSING via ./.venv/bin/python -m pytest (1 passed).

DISCRIMINATES: reverting CV-S-1 removes _int_env entirely (back to bare module-level int()), so the test errors with AttributeError. Even a non-naming helper would emit the bare-int message "invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'not-a-port'" (confirmed empirically), which does NOT match "ASSAY_TRACING_PORT must be an integer" — the test fails either way when reverted.

Minor, non-blocking: the parse still happens at import time (outside bootstrap_tracing's graceful-degradation handler), so a malformed value crashes import rather than degrading to untraced — but that is exactly the same scope/intent as the named #H-022-class fix, and the message is now diagnosable.
